segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
If you've had a dogman encounter
and would like to speak with me about it, whether in private or on the show, please
go to dogmeanencounters.com and submit a report.

Tonight's guest is Jeff.

Hi, how's it going?

Right around my 20th birthday, I enlisted in the U.S. Army.
Spent five years in active duty, another four years in active reserve.
All in the meantime, after I got out of active duty, I joined my local sheriff's office down over here.
Been working here ever since.
And I have a wife, I have one kid, and another one on the way.
And it's really about the gist of it. I can give up right now.
Well, thank you so much for your service to our country. I really appreciate it.
Sure thing.
Jeff, your encounter happened at a military base.
Are you at Liberty to tell us which base it was?
It was, back then it's called Fort Polk, but now it's called Fort Johnson for all the newer folk who are coming into the military.
It's in Louisiana, just north of Leesville.
Had anything happened to you on that base before you had that encounter that gave you the impression that something just might have been off?
um my house that we lived in before my daughter was born we had a bunch of like hauntings
it was very prominent you would have all these posts that we would see on facebook because we
it was a post that we had it was a facebook page called uh weird things for polk especially for

me at that point in time.

I was prepared for ghosts.

to my surroundings. Because back then, you know, as a brand new private, I wasn't thinking much

Sure thing. So this encounter happened in something that we call JRTC, or Joint Readiness
Training Command, Fort Polk, Louisiana.
Um, it was roughly around the end of August, early September.
We were getting ready to finish one of our rotations.

generally for us is about a month to about 45 days, give or take, depending
on the training regimen that we're going to be doing, depending on what our job is.
That's generally how it goes.
My unit was what we call a home unit. We're not called Geronimo, we're just the home unit.
We aren't in the field as often as Geronimo was. They are in the field
monthly, unfortunately for them. But for us, we would go every three to four weeks.
It would be up for a couple of weeks. But during this timeframe, we were gone for an entire month.
so in the late portions of August early September I had just finished a night shift So it was my job in the intel field where I was in charge of operating a system that
allowed us to work with drones to see the surroundings around us, keep an eye on the
enemy forces for op war and make sure that we're in the safe vicinity that was primarily my job
my lieutenant lieutenant s is what i would like to call her because she asked me to keep her name out
of it she uh she was primarily what we would call an f2 kind of like the red hat it's her job to
to think of how the enemy is going to attack my unit, which at the time was a field artillery battalion.
And there was one instance where we got called to go to a meeting for the brigade.
And this was set to go off at around 3.30, 4.30 in the morning.
I had just worked night shifts the night beforehand, so I was getting ready to get up.
And she told me, hey, I need you to get up.
I need you to get the truck ready.
We're going to SP off around 30 minutes.
We're going to head over to brigade to have this meeting.

We're getting ready to go.
So I got the truck ready, prepared, set.

like there was a storm incoming.
